title: Abstract
content: A series of mesoporous TiO 2 SBA-15, CuTiO 2 and CuTiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts were prepared by sol-gel synthesis for photoreduction of CO 2 with H 2 O to methanol.It was found that optimum amount of titanium loading of TiO 2 SBA-15 was 45 wt which exhibited higher photoreduction activity than pure TiO 2 .An addition of copper on TiO 2 or TiO 2 SBA-15 catalyst as cocatalyst was found to enhance the catalytic activity because copper serves as an electron trapper and prohibits the recombination of hole and electron.

title: Experimental
content: The synthesis of SBA-15 was performed according to the method reported by Zhao et al. 15.In a typical preparation, 4.0 g of P123 (Aldrich) was dissolved in 160 ml of 2 M HCl solution, which was stirred at 30 C for 1 h.Then 8.4 g of TEOS (Aldrich) was added, and the resulting solution was stirred at this temperature for 24 h.The mixture was subsequently raised to 90 C for 24 h.After filtration and washing, the solid product was dried at 100 C then calcined in a stream of air at 550 C for 5 h.Nanocrystalline titanium dioxide was prepared via sol-gel hydrolysis and condensation of an isopropanol solution of titanium tetraisopropoxide (Ti(OC 3 H 7 ) 4 , Aldrich) (TTIP) in the SBA-15 silica support.A detailed explanation of the synthesis procedure can be found van Grieken et al. 16.A solution with the required amount of TTIP in isopropanol to obtain 25-65 wt TiO 2 loading was added to SBA-15. After stirring the mixture for 45 min at room temperature, it was centrifuged and the recovered solid was dried at 100 C overnight and calcined at 550 C for 4 h. The synthesis of 2 wt CuTiO 2 was performed according to the method reported previously 12,14 where 2 wt of CuCl 2 (J.T. Baker) was added on the 1 h during the hydrolysis-pcondensation of titanium precursor.The (2 wt CuTiO 2 )SBA-15 sample was prepared by adding copper and titanium precursor during the hydrolysis-condensation to the SBA-15 silica support where the weight percentage of the TiO 2 in the catalyst was 45 wt.Both catalysts were preheated at 100 C to remove the adsorbed water over night and then calcined in 500 C for 3 h, also in air. The characterization methods including Low Angle X-ray diffraction (SAXS, SystemOsmic, PSAXS-USH-WAXS-002), Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku X-Ray Diffractometer MAX-2500 V, Cu-Ka radiation, k 1.54178A ), UV-Vis (Varian Lary 5E diode array spectrometer), and TEM (JEOL JEM-2000FX P microscope).The N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured at -196 C on a Qantachorme, Autosorb-1-C sorptometer.The surface areas were calculated by BET equation and the BJH pore size distributions were calculated form adsorption isotherm. The crystallite size (d XRD ) of the titanium oxide was determined by X-ray line broadening analysis (XLBA) for TiO 2 (101) peaks according to the Scherrer formula: where j is the particle shape factor taken as 0.9. A photocatalytic reactor was built in the laboratory.The detail schematic drawing of the experimental set-up is shown in S1.The photocatalytic reaction system was equipped with a 400 W medium-pressure metal halide lamp (Phillps HPA400) with a peak light intensity at 365 nm mounted on an inner irradiation quartz cell.The photoreduction of CO 2 with water was performed in 550 ml aqueous 0.1 N NaOH solution containing about 0.05 g catalyst at 42 C.The initial pH value of 0.1 N NaOH solution was approximately pH 13.Then, CO 2 was bubbled through the reactor for at least 30 min to purge air and to saturate the solution.The pH value of the CO 2 -saturated NaOH solution was nearly pH 7. To maintain CO 2 saturation during the reaction, CO 2 gas was bobbled into the reactor continuously, and the pH value of the solution was kept near pH 7 during the reaction.The gas product was analyzed by a gas chromatography (China Gas Chromatography 9800) with a packed column (Porapak, 3.5 m in length) and FID detector.

title: Results and discussion
content: Figures 1 and2 show XRD patterns of TiO 2 SBA-15 with various TiO 2 content, CuTiO 2 and CuTiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts.In the low angle range (2h 0.007-5, see Fig. 1), the XRD pattern of pure SBA-15 shows the two well resolved peaks that can be indexed as (1 1 0) and (2 0 0) diffractions where the main peak associated with (1 0 0) diffraction, typically observed around 2h 0.98which belong to the ordered hexagonal mesoporous structure (Fig. 2) 15,17.After loading TiO 2 , the intensity of the low angle XRD peaks corresponding to the hexagonal symmetry decreased and shifted slightly to higher angle with increasing TiO 2 loading indicated the distortion of the mesoporous framework was increased by the intercalated TiO 2 .The wide-angle XRD patterns of the TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts evidenced that anatase was the only titania crystalline phase present in all prepared materials, as indicated by the peak emerging at 2h 25.3associated with (1 0 1) anatase diffraction (shown in Fig. 2) where no rutile phase was detected in any case, attributable to the absence of the (1 1 0) rutile reflection at 2h 27.4.The average crystallite sizes of TiO 2 , listed in Table 1, were determined from the Scherrer equation.It was reported that anatase-rutile transition in pure TiO 2 shows up at calcination temperatures above 400 C 18.However, no rutile formation was observed in TiO 2 SBA-15 composite catalysts calcined at 550 C.This behavior could be attributed to the stabilizing effect of SBA-15 which enhanced the thermal stability of the anatase crystalline phase, as earlier suggested by other authors 18,19.Furthermore, although the crystallite size of the titanium increased with the increasing titanium loading, all of the TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts showed a smaller crystallite size of TiO 2 than unsupported TiO 2 The morphology of TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalyst is further evident from the TEM observation.Figure 3 shows the TEM image of 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 which shows both ordered parallel channels and disordered mesoporous texture.This result demonstrated TiO 2 nanocrystal incorporated with the mesoporous texture near the particle surface SBA-15 and lead to a disordered mesoporous region near the particle surface and an ordered hexagonal mesoporous region inner the particle.These results were well consistent with the BET surface area analysis and BJH pore size distribution obtained through adsorption curve. The BET surface area of 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 was 436 m 2 g which was about half of pure SBA-15 (835 m 2 g) and was about 7 times larger than unsupported TiO 2 12.As shown in Fig. 4, the SBA-15 had a uniform pore size of 6.5 nm.After loading titanium species, 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 exhibited a wider pore distribution ranging from 2-12 nm resulting by the partial destruction of the mesostructure of SBA-15 and disordered mesoporous structure formed by TiO 2 nanocrystal aggregates. The UV-Vis spectra for these TiO 2 SBA-15 and Cu TiO 2 SBA-15 samples are shown in Fig. 5.As shown in the figure, the absorption edge of mesoporous TiO 2 SBA-15 shifted to the long-wavelength region with increased titanium content.The UV-Vis spectra of 10 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 showed an absorption edge at short wavelength region (330 nm) which was related to the large amount of 5 coordinate titanium species on the surface of nanosized TiO 2 crystallite (1.4 nm, see Table 1) 20.As mentioned above, the crystallite size of TiO 2 in TiO 2 SBA-15 composite was increased with the increasing titanium loading whereas the influence of distorted titanium species was decreased with the rising TiO 2 particle size.Thereby, the 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 and 55 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 photocatalysts exhibited a better crystallization and an absorption edge at longer wavelength region approached that of bulk anatase 20. As reported by Tseng et al. 14, 2 wt CuTiO 2 photocatalyst showed an absorption edge at longer wavelength (370-500 nm) than unloaded TiO 2 samples indicating a good contact between TiO 2 and Cu cocatalyst.Similar result was obtained in the UV-Vis spectra of (2 wt CuTiO 2 ) Titania is a photoexcited catalyst.The bandgap of anatase form TiO 2 is 3.2 eV, making it a perfect candidate for UV illumination.From a thermodynamic perspective, transforming one mol of CO 2 into methanol requires 228 kJ.Six electrons are required to covert the C 4? of CO 2 to the C 2- of methanol.The overall reaction is written as follows: In this study we use 0.1 N NaOH solutions as the initial composition of the aqueous solution.The NaOH solution is used in our experiments for the following two reasons.First, the high concentration of OH ions in an aqueous solution act as strong hole-scavengers and form OH radicals, thereby reducing the recombination of hole-electron pairs.Second, caustic NaOH solution dissolves more CO 2 than does pure water 21. Figure 6 shows the time course of methanol generation by the mesoporous TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts with TiO 2 content ranging of 25-55 wt, (2 wt CuTiO 2 ) SBA-15, 2 wt CuTiO 2 and commercial TiO 2 (P25).In all cases, the yield of methanol was increasing with the irradiation time and stopped when light was turned off indicated that the methanol generation for CO 2 reduction with H 2 O was photocatalytic.Methane, formic acid and other hydrocarbons might have been generated, but in quantities too low to be detected.Anpo et al. 22 reported the gas phase photoreduction of CO 2 with H 2 O and suggested that CO 2 and H 2 O interacted with the excited (Ti 3? -O -) species and formed H atoms, OH radicals form H 2 O molecular and surface carbonyl radicals from CO 2 molecular which reacted to form CH 4 and CH 3 OH.They also demonstrated that the Ti-containing zeolites lead to the formation of CH 3 OH due to the high dispersion state of the titanium oxide species and large pore size, while the formation of CH 4 is found to be the major reaction on anatase TiO 2 powder.Furthermore, Tseng et al. 12,14 reported that CO 2 can be reduced in NaOH solution by CuTiO 2 photocatalysts to form methanol, the other products (methane, formic acid or other hydrocarbons) was not detected.In this case, the CO 2 and its dissolved species (carbonates) in aqueous NaOH solution were found much more effective to facilitate the formation of methanol.Shioya et al. 23 also observed a higher selectivity for CH 3 OH formation with these TiO 2 -containing mesoporous photocatalysts can be attributed to large amount of surface OH radical, which lead to a high selectivity for CH 3 OH formation.In our present study, the titanium oxides included within SBA-15 were prepared using a sol-gel method exhibit a highly dispersed titanium oxides and photocatalytic reactivity for the reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O in NaOH solution to form CH 3 OH. The average production rate of methanol increased as the following order: 25 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 TiO 2 (P25) ( 55 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 2 wt CuTiO 2 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 (2 wt CuTiO 2 )SBA-15.Yang et al. 24 reported that the photocatalytic activity of TiO 2 was improved by loading TiO 2 on mesoporous SBA-15 because of the enhanced thermal stability of the TiO 2 anatase phase on SBA-15 and prevention of the grain growth the TiO 2 crystalline.In addition, the mesoporous structure of SBA-15 could also promote diffusion and adsorption of reactants.However, when the titanium loading of sample was too low (25 wt), the structure distortion of low crystalline TiO 2 of TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalyst increased the electron-hole recombination rate and led to a low photocatalytic activity.Meanwhile, the low concentration of active TiO 2 sites on the surface of 25 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 catalyst was also responsible for the low photocatalytic activity.On the other hand, increasing titanium loading resulted in increasing the generation of active TiO 2 sites to react with reactants, consequently increases the methanol yield.Hence, the 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 and 55 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 catalysts gave a much higher than 25 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 catalyst and TiO 2 (P25).Zhang et al. 8 reported that the photocatalytic activity depends on the titania mean particle size with an optimum value of around 10 nm.However, other authors 19 claimed improved activity for particles below such a value.In this work, 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 photocatalyst (crystalline size 5.5 nm) appears to be the optimum value for the CO 2 photoreduction with H 2 O.The average methanol production rate over the reaction period of 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 photocatalyst was 627 lmolAg -1 Ah -1 which was about 3 times higher than commercial TiO 2 (P25).When the titanium loading was higher than the optimum amount, the excess titanium formed larger crystallize and destructed the mesoporous framework of SBA-15and showed a decreasing activity.Therefore, 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 photocatalyst was chosen to further investigate the photoreduction of CO 2 with H 2 O on TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalyst with cupper catalyst. As shown in Fig. 6, addition of Cu on the TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalyst was effective to promote the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction for methanol prodction where the copper clusters served as an electron trap to reduce the recombination rate of photo exited electron-hole pairs 12.The (2 wt CuTiO 2 )SBA-15 catalyst exhibited a highest photocatalytic activity which was about 1.2 times as compared as unsupported 2 wt CuTiO 2 prepared by solgel synthesis and about 1.1 times higher than 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 without copper cocatalyst.It should be noticed that the optimum loading of Cu cocatalyst on TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalyst might not as the same as that on TiO 2 .Since, the copper loading played an important role for the photocatalytic activity of CuTiO 2 12,25, future studies are needed to optimize the copper loading on TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalyst.

title: Conclusions
content: We preformed the photoreduction of CO 2 with H 2 O for methanol production on a series of TiO 2 , mesoporous TiO 2 SBA-15, CuTiO 2 and CuTiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts.It was found the thermal stability and grain growth of anatase TiO 2 crystallite was confined when loading titanium precursor (TTIP) on SBA-15 support by sol-gel synthesis.The loading amount of TiO 2 of mesoporous TiO 2 SBA-15 composite photocatalysts played a key role to control the crystallize size of the supported TiO 2 particles and the mesoporous structure of the catalyst.The 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 photocatalyst with crystalline size about 5.5 nm appeared to be the optimum value for the CO 2 photoreduction with H 2 O.The (2 wt CuTiO 2 ) SBA-15 catalyst exhibited a highest photocatalytic activity (627 lmolAg -1 Ah -1 ).The high catalytic performance was attributed to the synergetic effects of the well dispersed nanocrystallite anatase TiO 2 on mesoporous of SBA-15 and copper cocatalyst as an effective electron trapper. Fig. 1 Low angle XRD patterns of (a) SBA-15 (b) 45 wt TiO 2 SBA15 (c) 60 wt TiO 2 SBA15 Fig. 3 TEM image of 45 wt TiO 2 SBA15 Fig. 5 UV-Vis spectra of (a) 10 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 (b) 45 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 (c) 55 wt TiO 2 SBA-15 (d) (2 wt CuTiO 2 )SBA-15 (e) 2 wt CuTiO 2 The crystalline properties, band gap and photocatalytic methanol evolution rates of catalysts 13om Tseng et al.13 H.-C.
